Two days later, Don Quixote got up, and the first thing he did was go to

see his books; and, since he did not find the room where he had left it, he went from one place to another looking for it. He arrived where he used to have the door, and felt for it with his hands, and turned his eyes about everywhere, without saying a word; but, after a good while, he asked his housekeeper whereabouts the room of his books was. The housekeeper, who was already well instructed in what she was to answer, said:
What room, or what nothing, is your grace looking for? There is no longer any room or books in this house, because the devil himself carried it all away.

It was not the devil —replied the niece—, but an enchanter who came on a cloud one night, after the day your grace left here, and, dismounting from a serpent on which he was riding, entered the room, and I don't know what he did inside, for after a short while he went flying out through the roof, and left the house full of smoke; and, when we remembered to look at what he had done, we saw neither book nor room any more; only we remember very well, both the housekeeper and I, that at the time that evil old man was leaving, he said in a loud voice that, because of a secret enmity he had against the owner of those books and room, he was leaving the damage done to that house which would later be seen. He also said that his name was the sage Muñatón.
Frestón would be the word —said Don Quixote.
I don't know —replied the housekeeper— if he was called Frestón or Fritón; only I know that his name ended in tón.

So it is —said Don Quixote—; for that is an enchanter, a great enemy of mine, who bears me ill will, because he knows through his arts and letters that I must come, in the course of time, to fight in single battle with a knight whom he favors, and I must defeat him, without him being able to prevent it, and therefore he tries to cause me all the disappointments he can; and I tell him that poorly will he be able to contradict or avoid what is ordered by heaven.

Who doubts that? —said the niece—. But who puts your grace, uncle, into these quarrels? Would it not be better to stay peaceful in your house and not go about the world seeking fancy bread, considering that many go for wool and come back shorn?
Oh my niece —replied Don Quixote—, and how poorly you reckon! Before they shear me, I will have plucked and removed the beards of all who imagine touching me even on the tip of a single hair.
The two did not want to reply any more, because they saw his anger being kindled.

The case is, then, that he stayed at home very peacefully for fifteen days, without showing any desire to follow up on his first frolics, during which days he passed the most amusing stories with his two compadres the priest and the barber, about how the thing the world needed most was wandering knights and how chivalry should be resurrected in it. The priest sometimes contradicted him and other times agreed, because if he did not observe this artifice, there was no way to get along with him.

During this time, Don Quixote courted a neighboring farmer of his, a man of good standing —if this title can be given to one who is poor—, but with very little salt in his noggin. In short, he said so much, persuaded so much and promised, that the poor villain decided to go off with him and serve him as a squire. Among other things, Don Quixote told him to prepare to go with him willingly, because sometimes an adventure could befall him that would win, in the twinkling of an eye, some island, and he would leave him as governor of it. With these promises and others like these, Sancho Panza —for that was the farmer's name— left his wife and children and agreed to become the squire of his neighbor.

Then Don Quixote set about finding money; and, selling one thing and pawning another, and mishandling them all, he came to have a reasonable sum. He also provided himself with a shield, which he borrowed from a friend, and, patching up his broken helmet the best he could, he informed his squire Sancho of the day and hour he intended to set out on his journey, so that he might provide himself with what he saw he needed most of all. Above all he charged him to bring saddlebags; and he said he would, and that likewise he intended to bring a donkey he had very good, because he was not accustomed to walking much on foot. Don Quixote paused a little regarding the donkey, considering whether he remembered if any wandering knight had ever brought a squire mounted on a donkey, but none came to his memory; however, despite this, he decided to bring him, with the plan of providing him with more honorable knighthood whenever an opportunity arose, taking the horse from the first discourteous knight he met. He provided himself with shirts and with the other things he could, following the advice the innkeeper had given him; all of which being done and fulfilled, without Panza saying goodbye to his children and wife, nor Don Quixote to his housekeeper and niece, one night they left the village without anyone seeing them; in which they walked so much that at dawn they considered themselves safe from being found even if they were searched for.

Sancho Panza rode upon his pack-animal like a patriarch, with his saddlebags and his flask, and with great desire to already see himself as governor of the island his master had promised him. It happened that Don Quixote took the same route and road as the one he had taken on his first journey, which was through the field of Montiel, by which he traveled with less heaviness than the previous time, because, being the hour of morning and the sun's rays striking them obliquely, they did not fatigue them. At this, Sancho Panza said to his master:
Look, your grace, wandering knight, that you don't forget what you have promised me about the island; for I will know how to govern it, however big it may be.
To which Don Quixote replied:

You must know, friend Sancho Panza, that it was a very common custom of the ancient wandering knights to make their squires governors of the islands or kingdoms they won, and I have determined that by me this grateful custom shall not fail; rather, I plan to excel in it: because they sometimes, and perhaps most often, waited until their squires were old; and, after being tired of serving and of carrying bad days and worse nights, they gave them some title of count, or, at most, of marquis, of some valley or province more or less; but, if you live and I live, it could well be that before six days I might win such a kingdom that had others adhered to it, which would come in handy to crown you king of one of them. And don't think much of it, for things and случаи happen to such knights, by ways so never seen or thought of, that with ease I could give you even more than I promise you.
In that way —replied Sancho Panza—, if I were king by some miracle of those your grace mentions, at the very least, Juana Gutiérrez, my spouse, would become queen, and my children infantes.
Well, who doubts it? —replied Don Quixote.

I doubt it —replied Sancho Panza—; because I am of the opinion that, although God were to rain kingdoms upon the earth, none would sit well upon the head of Mari Gutiérrez. Know, sir, that she is not worth two maravedis for a queen; a countess would suit her better, and even God and help.
Commend it to God, Sancho —replied Don Quixote—, for He will give what best suits Him, but do not shrink your spirit so much that you come to content yourself with less than being an admiral.
I will not do so, my lord —replied Sancho—; all the more having such a principal master in your grace, who will know how to give me all that is proper for me and that I can bear.

chapters.title: Chapter VIII. Of the good fortune that the valiant Don Quijote had in the frightful and never-before-imagined adventure of the windmills, with other events worthy of happy remembrance
paragraphs: At this, they spied thirty or forty windmills that are in that field; and, as soon as Don Quijote saw them, he said to his squire:

" Fortune is guiding our affairs better than we could have wished, because you see there, friend Sancho Panza, where there appear thirty, or a few more, gargantuan giants, with whom I intend to do battle and take the lives of all of them, with whose spoils we shall begin to enrich ourselves; for this is good war, and it is a great service to God to remove such evil seed from off the face of the earth."
" What giants?" said Sancho Panza.
" Those that you see there," answered his master. "The long arms, which some of them usually have, of nearly two leagues."
" Look your worship," answered Sancho. "Those that appear there are not giants, but windmills, and what seem to be arms in them are the vanes, which, turned by the wind, make the millstone turn."

" Well it appears," answered Don Quijote, "that you are not experienced in this matter of adventures: they are giants; and if you are afraid, get away from there, and put yourself in prayer in the space that I am about to enter with them in fierce and unequal battle."

And, saying this, he spurred his horse Rocinante, without heeding the voices that his squire Sancho called out to him, warning him that, without any doubt, those he was about to attack were windmills, and not giants. But he was so convinced they were giants, that he neither heard the voices of his squire Sancho nor realized, although he was now quite close, what they were; rather, he went saying in loud voices:
" Do not flee, cowardly and vile creatures, for a single knight is the one who attacks you."
At this, a little wind rose and the great vanes began to turn, which Don Quijote, seeing, said:
" Even though you move more arms than the giant Briareus, you shall have to pay me for this."

And, saying this, and commending himself with all his heart to his lady Dulcinea, asking her to help him in such a strait, well covered by his shield, with his lance couched, he charged at full gallop of Rocinante and thrust at the first windmill that was in front; and, giving it a thrust in the vane, the wind turned it with such fury that it shattered the lance, taking with it the horse and the knight, who went rolling badly battered across the field. Sancho Panza came to help him, at full run of his donkey, and when he arrived he found that he could not move: such was the blow that Rocinante gave with him.

" God help me!" said Sancho. "Did I not tell your worship to look well what you were doing, that they were nothing but windmills, and you could not ignore it unless you had others like them in your head?"

" Hold your tongue, friend Sancho," answered Don Quijote, "for the things of war, more than others, are subject to constant change; moreover, I think, and it is true, that that sage Frestón [ANN: The enchanter who took his library] who stole my room and my books has turned these giants into windmills to take away from me the glory of their conquest: such is the enmity he bears me; but, in the end, in the end, his evil arts shall not be able to prevail against the goodness of my sword."
" May God make it so," answered Sancho Panza.

And, helping him to rise, he put him back on Rocinante, who was half-dislocated. And, talking about the past adventure, they continued on the road to Puerto Lápice, because there, Don Quijote said, it was impossible not to find many and diverse adventures, for it was a very frequented place; but he went very sorrowful because he had lost his lance; and, telling his squire about it, he said:

" I remember having read that a Spanish knight, called Diego Pérez de Vargas, when his sword had broken in battle, tore from an oak a heavy branch or trunk, and with it performed such deeds that day, and crushed so many Moors, that he was left with the surname Machuca, and thus he and his descendants were called, from that day forward, Vargas and Machuca. I have told you this, because from the first oak or oak tree that presents itself to me I intend to tear another trunk such and as good as that one, which I imagine and think to perform such feats with, that you shall consider yourself fortunate to have deserved to come to see them and to be witness to things that can scarcely be believed."

" To God's hand," said Sancho. "I believe everything just as your worship says it; but straighten yourself a bit, for it seems you are listing to one side, and it must be from the battering of the fall."

" That is the truth," answered Don Quijote; "and if I do not complain of the pain, it is because it is not given to wandering knights to complain of any wound, even if their guts were to come out of it."

" If that is so, I have nothing to argue," answered Sancho, "but may God know if I would have wished your worship to complain when something hurt you. For my part I must say that I shall complain of the smallest pain I have, unless that rule of not complaining also applies to the squires of wandering knights."

Don Quijote could not help laughing at the simplicity of his squire; and so, he declared to him that he could very well complain, as and when he wished, with or without reason; for until then he had read nothing to the contrary in the order of chivalry. Sancho told him it was time to eat. His master answered that for the time being he had no need; that he should eat whenever he pleased. With this license, Sancho arranged himself as best he could atop his donkey, and, taking from the saddlebags what he had put in them, he went walking and eating behind his master at his leisure, and from time to time he raised the wineskin, with such enjoyment, that the most pampered tavern-keeper of Málaga could have envied him. And, while he went in that manner taking frequent swigs, he did not remember any promise his master had made him, nor did he consider any hardship, but rather great rest, going about seeking adventures, however dangerous they might be.

In short, that night they passed among some trees, and from one of them Don Quijote tore a dry branch that could almost serve him as a lance, and placed on it the iron tip he had removed from the one that had broken on him. All that night Don Quijote did not sleep, thinking of his lady Dulcinea, in order to conform to what he had read in his books, when knights spent nights awake in forests and desolate places, entertained by the memories of their ladies. Sancho Panza did not spend it thus, for, having his stomach full, and not with chicory water, sleep took him all at once; and the sun's rays, which struck his face, nor the song of the birds, which, many and very joyfully, greeted the coming of the new day, could not have awakened him, had not his master called him. On getting up, he took a sip from the wineskin, and found it somewhat flatter than the night before; and his heart grew heavy, for it seemed to him they were not on a path to remedy its lack soon. Don Quijote did not want to breakfast, because, as has been said, he had taken to sustaining himself with delicious memories. They returned to their begun road to Puerto Lápice, and around three in the afternoon they spied it.

" Here," said Don Quijote, on seeing it, "we can, brother Sancho Panza, thrust our hands up to the elbows in this that they call adventures. But note that, although you see me in the greatest dangers in the world, you must not lay hand to your sword to defend me, unless you see that those who offend me are rabble and base people, in which case you may well help me; but if they are knights, it is by no means lawful nor granted to you by the laws of chivalry to help me, until you are armed a knight."

" Indeed, sir," answered Sancho, "your worship shall be well obeyed in this; and moreover, I of myself am peaceable and an enemy of getting into quarrels or fights. It is true that, in what concerns defending my own person, I shall not pay much heed to those laws, for both divine and human laws permit each one to defend himself from whoever wishes to harm him."
" I say no less," answered Don Quijote; "but, in this matter of helping me against knights, you must hold your natural impulses in check."
" I say I shall do so," answered Sancho, "and that I shall keep this precept as well as on the Sabbath."

While they were saying these words, two friars of the Order of Saint Benedict came along the road, riding on two dromedaries: for the mules they rode were no smaller. They had their traveling spectacles and their parasols. Behind them came a coach, with four or five horsemen who accompanied it and two muleteers on foot. In the coach, as was learned later, there was a Biscayan lady, who was going to Seville, where her husband was, who was leaving for the Indies with a very honorable position. The friars were not traveling with her, although they were on the same road; but hardly had Don Quijote spied them, when he said to his squire:

" Either I am mistaken, or this must be the most famous adventure that has ever been seen; for those black shapes that appear there must be, and are without doubt, some enchanters who are carrying off some princess in that coach, and I must undo this wrong with all my might."

" This will be worse than the windmills," said Sancho. "Look, sir, those are Benedictine friars, and the coach must be for some traveling people. Look, I say, that you look well what you do, let not the devil deceive you."
" I have already told you, Sancho," answered Don Quijote, "that you know little of the business of adventures; what I say is true, and now you shall see."

And, saying this, he pressed forward and positioned himself in the middle of the road by which the friars were coming, and, upon getting near enough that it seemed to him they could hear what he said, in a loud voice he said:
" Diabolical and monstrous folk, release immediately the noble princesses that you carry off by force in that coach; if not, prepare to receive swift death, as just punishment for your evil deeds."
The friars pulled in their reins, and were astonished, both at the figure of Don Quijote and at his words, to which they replied:
" Sir knight, we are neither diabolical nor monstrous, but two Benedictine friars going our way, and we do not know if there are any princesses carried off by force in that coach or not."
" For me there are no soft words, for I already know you, lying rabble," said Don Quijote.

And, without waiting for more reply, he spurred Rocinante and, with lance lowered, charged against the first friar, with such fury and boldness that, if the friar had not let himself fall from the mule, he would have made him come to the ground against his will, and even wounded, if not fallen dead. The second friar, who saw the way his companion was being treated, put legs to his good mule and began to run across that plain, faster than the wind itself.

Sancho Panza, who saw the friar on the ground, lightly dismounting from his donkey, rushed at him and began to take off his habit. At this, two of the friars' muleteers arrived and asked him why he was undressing him. Sancho answered that it belonged to him legitimately, as spoils from the battle that his lord Don Quijote had won. The muleteers, who did not know about jokes, nor understood that business of spoils or battles, seeing that Don Quijote was now away from there, talking with those who were in the coach, set upon Sancho and threw him to the ground; and, without leaving a hair on his beard, they beat him with kicks and left him stretched on the ground breathless and senseless. And, without stopping for a moment, the friar remounted, all fearful and cowardly and pale; and, when he found himself on horseback, he spurred after his companion, who was waiting for him at some distance, waiting to see how that alarm would turn out; and, without wanting to wait for the end of all that begun affair, they continued on their way, making more signs of the cross than if they had the devil at their backs.
Don Quijote was, as has been said, talking with the lady of the coach, saying to her:

" Your beauty, my lady, may do with her person what best pleases her, for the pride of your robbers now lies on the ground, thrown down by this my strong arm; and, so that you may not suffer not knowing the name of your liberator, know that my name is Don Quijote of La Mancha, a wandering knight and adventurer, and captive of the peerless and beautiful Lady Dulcinea of El Toboso; and, in payment of the benefit you have received from me, I desire nothing else but that you return to El Toboso, and that you present yourself before this lady and tell her what I have done for your liberty."

All this that Don Quijote was saying was heard by one of the squires accompanying the coach, who was a Biscayan; who, seeing that he did not want to let the coach pass, but rather said that he must immediately turn back to El Toboso, went up to Don Quijote and, seizing his lance, said to him, in bad Castilian tongue and worse Biscayan, in this manner:
" Go, knight who fare ill; by the God who made me, if you do not let go of the coach, I shall kill you as surely as you are a Biscayan there."
Don Quijote understood him very well, and with great calmness answered him:
" If you were a knight, as you are not, I would already have punished your foolishness and audacity, captive creature."
To which the Biscayan replied:

" Am I not a knight? I swear to God you lie as a Christian. If you throw lance and draw sword, how quickly you shall see that I carry the cat to the water! [ANN: A garbled threat/boast in broken Spanish, typical of the comic linguistic confusion between the two characters] Biscayan on land, hidalgo on sea, hidalgo by the devil; and you lie, look if you say something else."
" Now you shall see it, said Agrajes!" [ARC: An archaic expression, used as a challenge] answered Don Quijote.

And, throwing his lance to the ground, he drew his sword and covered himself with his shield, and charged the Biscayan with determination to take his life. The Biscayan, who saw him coming, although he would have liked to dismount from the mule, which, being one of the sorry rented ones, was not to be trusted, could do nothing else but draw his sword; but he was fortunate that he found himself next to the coach, from which he could take a cushion that served him as a shield, and then they went at each other, as if they were two mortal enemies. The rest of the people would have liked to make peace between them, but could not, for the Biscayan kept saying in his poorly-spoken words that if they did not let him finish his battle, he himself would kill his lady and all the people who stood in his way. The lady of the coach, astonished and fearful of what she saw, had the coachman move away from there a little, and from afar she set herself to watch the fierce contest, during which the Biscayan gave Don Quijote a great slash on one shoulder, over his shield, which, had it landed undefended, would have opened him to the waist. Don Quijote, who felt the weight of that enormous blow, gave a great shout, saying:
" Oh lady of my soul, Dulcinea, flower of beauty, come to the aid of this your knight, who, to satisfy your great goodness, finds himself in this rigorous strait!"

The saying this, and the gripping of the sword, and the covering himself well with his shield, and the charging of the Biscayan, all happened at one and the same time, carrying the determination to risk everything on a single blow.

The Biscayan, who saw him coming against him, understood well by his boldness his courage, and determined to do the same as Don Quijote: and so, he waited, well covered by his cushion, without being able to turn the mule to one side or the other; for, from sheer tiredness and not being used to such nonsense, she could not take a step.

Came then, as has been said, Don Quijote against the cunning Biscayan, with his sword raised high, with determination to split him in two, and the Biscayan waited for him likewise with sword raised and covered by his cushion, and all the bystanders were fearful and hanging on what was to come from those enormous blows with which they threatened each other; and the lady of the coach and her maids were making a thousand vows and offerings to all the images and places of devotion in Spain, that God might deliver her squire and them from that great danger in which they found themselves.

But the trouble with all this is that at this point and juncture the author of this history leaves this battle hanging, apologizing that he found nothing more written of these deeds of Don Quijote beyond what has been related. It is true that the second author of this work did not wish to believe that so curious a history had been delivered to the laws of oblivion, nor that the wits of La Mancha had been so careless as not to have in their archives or studies some papers that treated of this famous knight; and so, with this notion, he did not despair of finding the end of this pleasant history, which, the heavens being favorable, he did find in the manner that shall be told in the Second Part.
Second part of the ingenious hidalgo Don Quijote of La Mancha

chapters.titleEn: Chapter IX. Where the Stupendous Battle That the Gallant Biscayan and the Valiant Manchegan Had Is Concluded and Ended
paragraphs: We left in the first part of this history the valiant Biscayan and the famous Don Quixote with their swords raised and bare, ready to deliver two furious blows, such that, if they struck fully, at the very least they would be split and cleaved from top to bottom and opened like a pomegranate; and that at that so doubtful moment the story stopped and remained truncated, so savory, without its author giving us any news of where we might find what was missing from it.

This caused me great distress, because the pleasure of having read so little turned into displeasure, thinking of the difficult path that presented itself to find so much that, in my opinion, was missing from such a savory tale. It seemed to me an impossible thing and outside all good custom that such a good knight should have lacked some sage who took upon himself the task of writing his never-before-seen deeds, something that none of the knights-errant lacked.
of those whom people say go seeking their adventures.

because each of them had one or two sages, as if cast in a mold, who not only wrote their deeds, but also painted their most minute thoughts and childish antics, however hidden they might be; and it should not be so unfortunate for such a good knight, that he should lack what abounded in Platir and others like him. And so, I could not bring myself to believe that such a gallant story had remained maimed and crippled; and I blamed the malignancy of time, devourer and consumer of all things, which either had it hidden or had consumed it.

On the other hand, it seemed to me that, since among his books had been found such modern ones as Disengagement of Jealousy and Nymphs and Shepherds of Henares, that his story too must be modern; and that, even if it were not written down, it would be in the memory of the people of his village and of those surrounding it. This imagination left me confused and eager to know, truly and actually, the entire life and miracles of our famous Spanish Don Quixote of La Mancha, light and mirror of Manchegan knighthood, and the first who in our age and in these so calamitous times took on the toil and exercise of arms, and to undo grievances, succor widows, protect maidens, of those who went about with their whips and palfreys, and with all their virginity upon them, from mountain to mountain and from valley to valley; for, unless some scoundrel, or some villain with axe and cap, [NOTE: a type of medieval helmet] or some monstrous giant forced them, there was a maiden in times past who, after eighty years, during all of which she did not sleep one day under a roof, went to the grave as whole as the mother who bore her. I say, then, that for these and many other reasons, our gallant Quixote is worthy of continual and memorable praises; and even to me they should not be denied, for the toil and diligence I put into seeking the end of this pleasant story; although I well know that if heaven, chance, and fortune do not help me, the world will be lacking and without the pastime and pleasure that for almost two hours he who reads it with attention may have. The finding of it came about in this manner:

One day while I was in the Alcaná of Toledo, a boy arrived to sell some manuscripts and old papers to a silk merchant; and, since I am fond of reading, even the torn papers in the streets, carried by this my natural inclination, I took a manuscript from among those the boy was selling, and saw that it was in characters I recognized as Arabic. And, although I recognized them, I did not know how to read them, I went about looking if some Morisco [ARC: Christian of Moorish descent] who could read them would appear there; and it was not very difficult to find such an interpreter, for, even if I had searched in a better and more ancient language, I would have found one. In short, chance provided me with one, who, upon my telling him my desire and placing the book in his hands, opened it in the middle, and, reading a little in it, began to laugh.

I asked him what he was laughing at, and he answered me that he was laughing at a thing that book had written in the margin by way of annotation. I told him to tell it to me; and he, without ceasing his laughter, said:

"It says here, as I have said, written in the margin this: 'This Dulcinea of Toboso, so often referred to in this history, they say had the best hand at salting pigs of any woman in all of La Mancha.'"

When I heard "Dulcinea of Toboso" mentioned, I was astounded and taken aback, because immediately it came to my mind that those manuscripts contained the history of Don Quixote. With this thought, I urged him to read the beginning, and, doing so, turning the Arabic into Castilian, he said it read: History of Don Quixote of La Mancha, written by Cide Hamete Benengeli, Arabian historian. Much discretion was needed to hide the contentment I received when the title of the book reached my ears; and, slipping past the silk merchant, I bought all the papers and manuscripts from the boy for half a real; [ANN: a small Spanish coin] for, if he had had discretion and known how much I desired them, he could well have promised and received more than six reales for the purchase. I then withdrew with the Morisco into the cloister of the main church, and begged him to translate all those manuscripts, all that dealt with Don Quixote, into Castilian, without taking away or adding anything, offering him whatever payment he wished. He was content with two arrobas [ANN: a unit of weight, about 25 pounds] of raisins and two fanegas [ANN: a dry measure, about 1.5 bushels] of wheat, and promised to translate them well and faithfully and with great speed. But I, to further facilitate the business and not to let slip such a good find, brought him to my house, where in a little more than a month and a half he translated the whole thing, in the same manner as is told here.

In the first manuscript, painted very naturally, was the battle of Don Quixote with the Biscayan, in the same posture the history tells, their swords raised, the one covered by his shield, the other by the cushion, and the Biscayan's mule so lifelike that it seemed to be showing it was for hire at crossbow range. At his feet the Biscayan had written a title that said: Don Sancho de Azpetia, which must undoubtedly have been his name, and at the feet of Rocinante was another that said: Don Quixote. Rocinante was wonderfully painted, so long and stretched out, so attenuated and lean, with such a spine, so confirmedly consumptive, that it clearly showed with how much care and propriety the name of Rocinante had been given him. Beside him was Sancho Panza, holding his donkey by the halter, at whose feet was another label that said: Sancho Zancas, and it must have been so because, as the painting showed, he had a big belly, short stature, and long shanks; and for this he must have been given the name of Panza and of Zancas, by which two surnames the history sometimes calls him. There were other small details worth noting, but all are of little importance and do not affect the true account of the history; for none is bad provided it be true.

If to this one can be raised some objection regarding its truth, it can be none other than that its author was an Arab, for it is very characteristic of those of that nation to be liars; although, because they are such our enemies, one can sooner understand that it has been lacking in it than excessive. And so it seems to me, for, when he could and should have extended his pen in the praises of such a good knight, it seems that of industry he passes them over in silence: a thing ill done and ill thought, there being and being obligated to be historians exact, truthful, and not at all biased, and that neither interest nor fear, rancor nor affection, should make them turn from the path of truth, whose mother is history, rival of time, deposit of actions, witness of what is past, example and notice of what is present, warning of what is to come. In this one I know will be found all that one might wish for in the most pleasing; and if something good should be lacking in it, I hold that it was the fault of the author's greyhound [ANN: meaning he was swift but came too late or left things out], rather than any deficiency in the subject. In short, its second part, following the translation, began in this manner:

Set and raised on high the sharpening swords of the two valiant and angry combatants, nothing could be seen but that they were threatening heaven, earth, and the abyss: such was the courage and bearing they had. And the first to come down with the blow was the choleric Biscayan, which was given with such force and such fury that, had his sword not turned aside in its path, that single blow alone would have been enough to end his rigorous combat and all the adventures of our knight; but good fortune, which had him reserved for greater things, twisted his opponent's sword aside, so that, although it struck him on the left shoulder, it did him no other harm than to disarm that whole side, carrying away in the bargain a great part of his helmet, with half the ear; all of which with frightful ruin came to the ground, leaving him very badly battered.

God help me, who will be able to adequately tell now the rage that entered the heart of our Manchegan, seeing himself stopped in such a manner! Let nothing more be said, except that it was such that he rose again in his stirrups, and, gripping the sword more tightly in both hands, with such fury he came down upon the Biscayan, striking him fully on the cushion and on the head, so that, no matter how good such a defense, as if a mountain had fallen upon him, he began to bleed from his nose, and from his mouth and ears, and to show signs of falling from his mule below, whence he would certainly have fallen had he not clung to its neck; but, for all that, he took his feet out of the stirrups and then let go his arms; and the mule, frightened by the terrible blow, ran across the field, and after a few capers threw its owner to the ground.

Don Quixote was watching it all with great calmness, and, when he saw him fall, he leapt from his horse and with great agility went to him, and, placing the point of his sword before his eyes, told him to surrender; otherwise, he would cut off his head. The Biscayan was so confused that he could not utter a word, and he would have fared badly, so blind with rage was Don Quixote, had the ladies of the coach, who until then had been watching the fight with great faint-heartedness, not gone to where he was and begged him with great urgency to do them the great mercy and favor of sparing the life of their squire.

"Truly, lovely ladies, I am very content to do what you ask of me; but it must be under one condition and agreement, which is that this knight must promise me to go to the town of Toboso and present himself on my behalf before the peerless Lady Dulcinea, so that she may do with him as best be her will."

The fearful and disconsolate lady, without entering into account of what Don Quixote was asking, and without asking who Dulcinea might be, promised him that the squire would do everything that was commanded on his behalf.
"Then, on the faith of that word, I will do him no more harm, even though I had well deserved it."

chapters.titleEn: Chapter X. Of what most happened to Don Quixote with the Biscayan, and of the danger he found himself in with a band of Yanguesian carriers
paragraphs: At that time Sancho Panza had already gotten up, somewhat mistreated by the boys of the friars, and had been attentive to the battle of his master Don Quixote, and he prayed to God in his heart that He would be pleased to give him victory and that in it he might win some island to make him governor of, as had been promised to him. Seeing, then, the scuffle finished and his master remounting Rocinante, he went to hold the stirrup for him; and before he mounted he knelt before him, and, taking hold of his hand, he kissed it and said to him:

May your grace be pleased, my lord Don Quixote, to give me the government of the island that was won in this rigorous scuffle; for, however large it may be, I feel myself with the strength to govern it such and as well as any other who has governed islands in the world.
To which Don Quixote responded:

Note, brother Sancho, that this adventure and those similar to it are not island adventures, but crossroads adventures, in which nothing is gained other than a torn head or one ear less. Have patience, for adventures will arise where I may not only make you governor, but more in the future.

Sancho thanked him much, and, kissing his hand again and the skirt of the lorica, he helped him mount Rocinante; and he mounted his donkey and began to follow his master, who, at a rapid pace, without bidding farewell or speaking further with those of the coach, entered a forest that was right there beside him. Sancho followed at the full trot of his beast, but Rocinante walked so much that, seeing himself left behind, he was forced to shout to his master to wait for him. Don Quixote did so, holding Rocinante's reins until his tired squire arrived, who, upon reaching him, said to him:

It seems to me, sir, that it would be wise for us to take shelter in some church; for, since the one you fought with was so badly damaged, it would not be surprising if they reported the matter to the Holy Brotherhood and they arrest us; and faith, if they do, before we leave the jail the hide will be sweating off us.
Hush, said Don Quixote. And where have you seen or read that any wandering knight has been brought before justice, no matter how many homicides he may have committed?

I know nothing of little murders, Sancho responded, nor have I ever examined any in my life; only I know that the Holy Brotherhood has jurisdiction over those who fight in the fields, and in the other I do not meddle.

Then have no worry, friend, Don Quixote responded, for I will deliver you from the hands of the Chaldeans, let alone from those of the Brotherhood. But tell me, for your life: have you ever seen a more valiant knight than I in all the discovered world? Have you read in histories of another who has had or had more spirit in undertaking, more breath in persevering, more skill in striking, or more cunning in overthrowing?

May the truth be, Sancho responded, that I have never read any history, because I neither know how to read nor write; but what I would dare wager is that I have never served a bolder master than your grace in all the days of my life, and may God will that these boldnesses not be paid for where I have said. What I beg of your grace is that you let yourself be treated, for you are losing much blood from that ear; for I have lint here in the saddlebags and some white ointment.
All that would have been well unnecessary, Don Quixote responded, if I had remembered to make a flask of the balsam of Fierabrás, for with a single drop time and medicines would have been saved.
What flask and what balsam is that? said Sancho Panza.

It is a balsam, Don Quixote responded, whose recipe I have in memory, with which there is no need to fear death, nor to think of dying from any wound. And so, when I make it and give it to you, you need only do this: when you see that in some battle they have cut me in half (as often happens), neatly the part of the body that has fallen to the ground, and with great subtlety, before the blood congeals, place it on the other half that remains in the saddle, taking care to fit it together evenly and exactly; then give me to drink only two sips of the balsam I have mentioned, and you will see me remain healthier than an apple.

If that is so, Panza said, I renounce here and now the government of the promised island, and I want nothing else, in payment of my many good services, except that your grace give me the recipe of that supreme liquor; for I hold that an ounce of it will be worth more than two reales anywhere, and I need nothing more to pass this life honorably and at ease. But it should be known if it costs much to make.
For less than three reales three jugs can be made, Don Quixote responded.
Wretched me! Sancho replied. Then what is your grace waiting for to make it and teach it to me?
Hush, friend, Don Quixote responded, for greater secrets I intend to teach you and greater favors to do you; and for now, let us treat ourselves, for the ear hurts me more than I would wish.

Sancho drew from the saddlebags lint and ointment. But when Don Quixote came to see his helmet broken, he thought he would lose his mind, and, placing his hand on his sword and raising his eyes to heaven, he said:

I make oath to the Creator of all things and to the four holy Gospels, where they are written most at length, to live the life the great Marquis of Mantua lived when he swore to avenge the death of his nephew Valdovinos, which was to eat no bread at tablecloths, nor lie with his wife, and other things which, although I do not remember them all, I take as expressed here, until I take full vengeance on the one who did me such an outrage.
Hearing this, Sancho said to him:

Let your grace note, Lord Don Quixote, that if the knight has fulfilled what he was ordered to do, namely to go present himself before my lady Dulcinea of El Toboso, he will already have fulfilled what he owed, and deserves no other punishment unless he commits a new crime.

You have spoken and reasoned very well, Don Quixote responded; and thus, I annul the oath as far as taking new vengeance from it is concerned; but I make and confirm it anew to live the life I have said, until I take by force another helmet as good as this one from some knight. And do not think, Sancho, that I am doing this on a whim, for I have someone to imitate in this; the very same thing happened, to the letter, with the helmet of Mambrino, which cost Sacripante so dearly.

May your grace give such oaths to the devil, my lord, Sancho replied; for they are very harmful to health and very prejudicial to conscience. If not, tell me now: if by chance we do not encounter any armed man with a helmet for many days, what are we to do? Is the oath to be fulfilled, in spite of so many inconveniences and discomforts, such as sleeping dressed, and not sleeping in populated areas, and a thousand other penances that that mad old man of the Marquis of Mantua contained in his oath, which your grace now wishes to ratify? Let your grace consider well that along all these roads there are no armed men, only muleteers and carters, who not only do not carry helmets, but perhaps have never heard them named in all their lives.
You are mistaken about that, Don Quixote said, for we will not have been two hours at these crossroads when we see more armed men than those who came upon Albraca to conquer fair Angélica.
Well then, so be it, Sancho said, and may it please God that things go well for us, and that the time to win this island that costs me so dearly may come soon, and let me die then.

I have already told you, Sancho, that this should not worry you at all; for, when there is a lack of an island, there is the kingdom of Denmark or that of Soli­disa, which will fit you like a ring; and more, since it is on firm land, you should rejoice the more. But let us leave this for its time, and see if you have anything in those saddlebags that we may eat, for we are to go shortly in search of some castle where we may lodge tonight and make the balsam I have told you about; for I swear to God that my ear is hurting me very much.
Here I have an onion, and a bit of cheese, and I don't know how many crusts of bread, Sancho said, but they are not dishes fitting for so valiant a knight as your grace.

How poorly you understand! Don Quixote responded. Let me inform you, Sancho, that it is the honor of wandering knights not to eat for a month; and, when they do eat, let it be of what they find most at hand; and this would have been certain to you if you had read as many histories as I have; for, although they have been many, in all of them I have not found any account of wandering knights eating, except by chance and at some sumptuous banquets that were made for them, and the other days they passed in flowers. And, although it may be understood that they could not go without eating and without performing all the other natural necessities, because, in truth, they were men like us, it must also be understood that, spending most of the time of their lives wandering through forests and uninhabited places, and without a cook, their most ordinary fare would be rustic viands, such as those you now offer me. So, friend Sancho, let not what gives me pleasure trouble you. Nor wish to make a new world, nor to displace wandering chivalry from its hinges.

May your grace pardon me, Sancho said; for, as I cannot read nor write, as I have said before, I do not know nor have I fallen into the rules of the chivalric profession; and from now on, I will stock the saddlebags with all kinds of dried fruit for your grace, who is a knight, and for myself, since I am not one, with other volatile things and of more substance.

I do not mean, Sancho, Don Quixote replied, that it is obligatory for wandering knights to eat nothing but those fruits you mention, but that their most ordinary sustenance should be of them, and of some herbs they find in the fields, which they know and I also know.
It is a virtue, Sancho responded, to know those herbs; for, as I am beginning to imagine, some day it will be necessary to make use of that knowledge.

And, upon taking out, at that point, what he said he had brought, the two ate in good peace and company. But, desiring to find where to lodge that night, they finished their meager and dry meal with great haste. They then mounted their horses, and hurried to reach populated area before nightfall; but the sun failed them, and the hope of attaining what they desired, beside some huts of some goatherds, and thus, they determined to spend the night there; for, as much as it was a source of sorrow for Sancho not to reach populated area, it was a cause of contentment for his master to sleep it under the open sky, for it seemed to him that each time this happened it was to perform a possessive act that would facilitate the proof of his knighthood.

chapters.titleEn: Chapter XI. Of what happened to Don Quixote with some goatherds
paragraphs: He was received by the goatherds with good cheer; and, having Sancho, as well as he could, settled Rocinante and his donkey, he went after the smell that certain chunks of goat were giving off, boiling over the fire in a cauldron; and, although he would have liked at that very moment to see if they were ready to transfer them from the cauldron to his stomach, he refrained from doing so, because the goatherds had removed them from the fire, and, spreading some sheepskins on the ground, they hastily prepared their rustic table and invited the two of them, with signs of very good will, to share what they had. They sat around the skins, six of them, the ones that were in the fold, having first, with crude ceremonies, begged Don Quixote to sit on a halved barrel that they placed upside down for him. Don Quixote sat down, and Sancho remained standing to serve him the cup, which was made of horn. Seeing his master in standing position, he said:

"So that you may see, Sancho, the good that knighthood holds within itself, and how close those who exercise any ministry of it are to soon being honored and esteemed in the world, I want you to sit here beside me and in the company of these good people, and that you be one same thing with me, who am your master and natural lord; that you eat from my plate and drink from where I drink; for of knighthood one may say the same as is said of love: that all things it equalizes."

"Great mercy!" said Sancho; "but I can tell Your Grace that, as long as I have good food to eat, I would eat it just as well and better standing and alone as sitting next to an emperor. And even, if truth be told, what I eat in my corner tastes much better to me, without squeamishness or regards, even if it is bread and onion, than the turkeys of other tables where I must necessarily chew slowly, drink little, wipe myself often, not sneeze or cough if I feel like it, nor do other things that solitude and freedom bring with them. So, my lord, these honors that Your Grace wishes to give me for being a minister and adherent of knight-errantry, as I am being Your Grace's squire, convert them into other things that are more comfortable and profitable for me; for although I receive them as well pleased, I renounce them from here to the end of the world."
"Nevertheless, you must sit; for whom humbles himself, God exalts."
And, seizing him by the arm, he forced him to sit beside him.

The goatherds did not understand that gibberish of squires and knights-errant, and did nothing but eat and be silent, and look at their guests, who, with great grace and appetite, were devouring chunks of meat the size of a fist. When the meat course was finished, they spread on the fleeces a great quantity of hazelnut acorns, and together they placed a half-cheese, harder than if it had been made of mortar. Meanwhile, the horn was not idle, for it went around so often (now full, now empty, like a well-bucket of a noria) that it easily emptied one of the two wineskins that were in plain sight. After Don Quixote had well satisfied his stomach, he took a handful of acorns in his hand, and, looking at them attentively, gave voice to such words:

"Happy age and happy centuries those that the ancients called golden, and not because gold, which in this our iron age is so highly esteemed, was obtained in that fortunate age without any toil, but because those who lived in it were ignorant of these two words of 'yours' and 'mine.' [NOTE: The phrase ' tuyo y mío' represents the concept of private property, which Cervantes presents as the origin of social problems.] In that blessed age all things were in common; no one needed, to obtain their ordinary sustenance, to take any other labor than to raise their hand and reach it from the sturdy oaks, which liberally were inviting them with their sweet and ripe fruit. The clear springs and running rivers, in magnificent abundance, offered them tasty and transparent waters. In the crevices of the rocks and in the hollow of the trees, the industrious and discreet bees formed their republic, offering to any hand, without any interest, the fertile harvest of their sweetest labor. The valiant cork oaks shed, without any other artifice than that of their courtesy, their broad and light bark, with which houses began to be covered, supported on rustic stakes, no more than for defense against the inclemencies of the sky. It was all peace then, all friendship, all concord; the heavy share of the curved plow had not yet dared to open nor visit the merciful entrails of our first mother, who, without being forced, offered, from all parts of her fertile and spacious bosom, what could fill, sustain and delight the children that then possessed her. Then indeed did the simple and beautiful shepherdesses wander from valley to valley and from hillock to hillock, with braids and with flowing hair, wearing no more clothing than was needed to honestly cover what honesty has always wanted and still wants covered; and their adornments were not of those now in use, which the purple of Tyre [ARC: a luxurious fabric dyed purple, extremely expensive] and the silk martyred in so many ways extol, but of some green leaves of burdock [ARC: a plant with large leaves, used here as simple decoration] and ivy woven together, with which perhaps they went as pompous and adorned as our court ladies now go with the rare and strange inventions that idle curiosity has shown them. Then the amorous concepts of the soul were decorated simply and plainly, in the same way and manner as she conceived them, without seeking artful circumlocution of words to extol them. Fraud, deceit, nor malice had not yet mixed themselves with truth and simplicity. Justice stood in its own terms, without those of favor and interest daring to disturb or offend it, who now so diminish, disturb, and persecute it. The law of the loop [ARC: 'ley del encaje' was a legal term for technicalities and loopholes used to circumvent true justice] had not yet seated itself in the understanding of the judge, because there was then nothing to judge, nor anyone to be judged. The maidens and honesty walked, as I have said, everywhere, alone and mistress, without fear that another's boldness and lascivious intent might diminish it, and their downfall arose from their own pleasure and free will. And now, in these our detestable centuries, none is safe, even if another new labyrinth like that of Crete [NOTE: The Labyrinth of Crete housed the Minotaur and represented an inescapable prison] conceals and locks her; for there, through the cracks or through the air, with the zeal of cursed solicitude, the amorous pestilence enters them and makes them ruin all their modesty. For whose security, as times advanced and malice grew, the order of knights-errant was instituted, to defend maidens, protect widows, and succor orphans and the needy. Of this order am I, brother goatherds, whom I thank for the gladness and good welcome that you give to me and my squire; for, although by natural law all who live are obligated to favor knights-errant, still, because you received and regaled me without knowing this obligation, it is fitting that, with the will possible to me, I thank you for yours."

All this long harangue —which could very well have been spared— our knight said because the acorns they gave him brought to his memory the golden age, and he took it into his head to make that useless speech to the goatherds, who, without answering him a word, amazed and enraptured, were listening to him. Sancho, likewise, was silent and ate acorns, and visited very often the second wineskin, which they had hanging from a cork oak so that the wine would not get warm.
Don Quixote took longer to speak than it took to finish dinner; at the end of which, one of the goatherds said:

"So that Your Grace may more truly say, sir knight-errant, that we receive you with prompt and good will, we wish to give him pleasure and contentment by having a companion of ours sing, who will not be long in arriving; he is a very clever and very enamored youth, and who, above all, knows how to read and write and is a musician on a rebec, [ARC: a small three-stringed instrument, similar to a violin] which there is nothing more to desire."

Scarcely had the goatherd finished saying this, when the sound of the rebec reached his ears, and shortly after arrived the one who played it, who was a young man of about twenty-two years, of very good grace. His companions asked him if he had supped, and when he answered yes, the one who had made the offers said to him:

"In that way, Antonio, you can do us the favor of singing a little, so that this lord guest we have may see that there are also those in the hills and forests who know about music. We have told him of your good talents, and wish you to display them and prove us true; and so, I beg you by your life to sit and sing the romance of your loves that your uncle the benefice [ARC: a church official who receives a benefice or church income] composed for you, which has seemed very good in the village."
"I am pleased," answered the young man.
And, without further urging, he sat down on the trunk of a pollarded oak, and tuning his rebec, shortly after, with very good grace, began to sing, saying in this manner:
Antonio

"'I know, Olalla, that you adore me, even though you have not told me so, not even with your eyes alone, the mute tongues of love affairs. Because I know that you are wise, in that you love me I affirm myself; that love was never unfortunate that was known. It is quite true that at times, Olalla, you have given me indication that you have a bronze soul and your white breast of rock. But among your reproaches and most honest evasions, sometimes hope shows the edge of her dress. My faith leaps at the lure, which has never been able to diminish for not being called, nor grow for being chosen. If love is courtesy, from that which you have I infer that the end of my hopes must be as I imagine. And if services are part of making a kind heart, some of those I have done strengthen my cause. Because if you have looked at it, more than once you will have seen that I have dressed on Mondays what honored me on Sunday. Since love and elegance walk the same path, at all times to your eyes I wished to show myself polished. I left dancing because of you, nor do I mention the music you have listened to at odd hours and at the crow of the rooster. I do not count the praises I have spoken of your beauty; for, though true, they have caused some to speak ill of me. Teresa del Berrocal, I praising you, said to me: 'Such thinks he adores an angel, and comes to adore a monkey; thanks to the many trinkets and false hair, and hypocritical beauties, that deceive Love himself.' I denied it and she was angry; her cousin defended her: challenged me, and you know what I did and he did. I do not love you in bulk, nor do I court and serve you for the matter of concubinage; for my design is nobler. The Church has constraints that are tied with silk; put your neck in the yoke; you will see how I put mine. Wherefore, from here I swear, by the most blessed saint, not to leave these mountains except as a Capuchin."

With this the goatherd ended his song; and although Don Quixote begged him to sing a little more, Sancho Panza would not have it, because he was more for sleeping than for hearing songs. And so, he said to his master:
"Your Grace may as well settle right away where you are to lodge tonight, for the labor these good men have all day does not permit them to spend the nights singing."
"I understand you, Sancho," answered Don Quixote; "for it is clear to me that the visits of the wineskin demand more compensation in sleep than in music."
"It tastes good to all of us, blessed be God," answered Sancho.

"I do not deny it," replied Don Quixote, "but do you settle where you wish, for those of my profession look better awake than sleeping. But with all this, it would be well, Sancho, that you cure this ear of mine again, which hurts me more than it should."

Sancho did as he was commanded; and when one of the goatherds saw the wound, he told him not to worry, for he would provide a remedy with which it would easily heal. And, taking some leaves of rosemary, which abounded there, he chewed them and mixed them with a little salt, and, applying them to the ear, bandaged it very well, assuring him he would need no other medicine; and so it was.
